coredata.dc:title: Enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2 evolution on the novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots/CuBi2O4 microrods composite
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Guo, Feng
Li, Mingyang
Shi, Yu
Wu, Mingfang
Tang, Yubin
coredata.dc:description: A novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) modified CuBi2O4 photocatalyst was prepared through a facile hydrothermal method, and applied to the photocatalytic water splitting under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). Results exhibited the increased photocatalytic hydrogen production of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 compared with pure CuBi2O4 and CDs/CuBi2O4. The excellent photocatalytic performance of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite was attributed to efficient synergistic effect between N-CDs and CuBi2O4, which increased light harvesting and electron transfer capacity. Additionally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite was proposed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuBi2O4
N-CDs
CDs
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and N-CDs powders
Synthesis of CuBi2O4, CDs/CuBi2O4 and N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhangjiagang Science and Technology Support Program
Social Development) of Jiangsu Province , China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Zhangjiagang Science and Technology Support Program (Social Development) of Jiangsu Province , China (grant numbers: ZKS1510 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) FT-IR spectra of the CDs and N-CDs. (c) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of N-CDs. The XPS spectra of N-CDs: (d) survey; (e) C 1s high-resolution spectrum; (f) N 1s high-resolution spectrum. Inset of Fig. 1a shows digital photos of aqueous N-CDs (left) and CDs (right).
XRD patterns of CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO.
SEM images of (a) CBO and (b) N-CDs/CBO; (c) TEM image of N-CDs/CBO; (d) HAADF-STEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping images of N-CDs/CBO. Scale bar: 200 nm.
XPS spectra of N-CDs/CBO: (a) survey; (b) Cu 2p; (c) Bi 4f; (d) O 1s; (e) C 1s; (f) N 1s.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO.
(a) Typical time courses of H2 evolutions over as-prepared samples. (b) Four cycling runs of H2 evolution over N-CDs/CBO.
(a) PL spectra and (b) EIS curves of CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO.
Possible mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the N-CDs/CBO photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic H2 evolution on the novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots/CuBi2O4 microrods composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) modified CuBi2O4 photocatalyst was prepared through a facile hydrothermal method, and applied to the photocatalytic water splitting under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm). Results exhibited the increased photocatalytic hydrogen production of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 compared with pure CuBi2O4 and CDs/CuBi2O4. The excellent photocatalytic performance of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite was attributed to efficient synergistic effect between N-CDs and CuBi2O4, which increased light harvesting and electron transfer capacity. Additionally, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Extensive attentions have been received on using semiconductor photocatalysis that can directly converse solar energy to chemical energy to alleviate the global energy crisis, which is one of the most promising technologies to produce large-scale hydrogen from water [1–3]. Since the previous work of Fujishima and Honda in 1972, various kinds of photocatalysts (e.g. oxides [4,5], nitrides [6,7], and sulfides [8], ect.) have been made for producing hydrogen from water [9]. Despite the improvement in photocatalysis, several challenges still inevitably exist: (i) the limitation of visible light utilization due to wide band gaps (such as TiO2, ZnO, SnO2) [10,11]; (ii) the complicatedly synthetized process [12]; (iii) the high-cost precursors [13]; (iv) the toxicity and instability [14]. Therefore, the central issue of current research is committed to the exploration and design of high-efficient, stable, cost-effective visible-light photocatalysts.

Up to now, a multitude of bismuth-based metal compounds (e.g. Bi2WO6 [15], BiVO4 [16,17], Bi2MoO6 [18], Bi2O3 [19] and so forth) have been widely applied in photocatalytic field. Among of them, CuBi2O4 with spinel structure, as a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst, has received much attention lately. Firstly, CuBi2O4 can fully utilize the portion of visible light in the spectrum of solar owing to a suitable band gap in the range of 1.5–1.8 eV [20]. Secondly, its redox potential of the conduction band is more negative than that of H+/H2, meaning that CuBi2O4 has strong reduction potential to photocatalytic water splitting [21]. However, the single CuBi2O4 exhibited poor photocatalytic activity because of its fast recombination of electron-hole pairs [22]. In order to boost the photocatalytic activity of CuBi2O4, various methods have been applied to promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, such as controlling different morphologies [23,24], doping with metal ions [25] and constructing CuBi2O4-based composite photocatalysts [26,27].

Besides the previously reported strategies, using small-sized quantum dots modified semiconductors to form the heterostructures has also been considered as an effective strategy to accelerate the interfacial charge transfer, especially for carbon dots (CDs). This is because CDs possess a great deal of fascinating advantages such as innocuity, chemical stability, mass production and low budget [28,29]. On the one hand, CDs with dimensions of below 10 nm are low-cost and ecofriendly zero-dimensional nano-carbon material, implying the possibility of the large-scale production [30,31] On the other hand, CDs can exhibit the excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties in the CDs-containing composites due to its conjugated π structure [32]. In addition, CDs also possess robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching and good biocompatibility. These outstanding properties attract many researchers to utilize CDs modified semiconductors, such as Ag3PO4 [33], Fe2O3 [34], CoO [35], ect, to enhance their photocatalytic activity. Even so, improving the performance of CDs and widening the application of CDs are still a hot topic. In recent years, nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials (such as nanotubes, graphene, etc.) have received widespread attention, because nitrogen doping can effectively induce the delocalization of charges and regulate the overall function of carbon materials [36]. Consequently, the design of such a nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) modified CuBi2O4 to construct a highly efficient composite photocatalyst is worth of expectation. To the best of our knowledge, few reports on the N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite have been reported.

In this study, a novel N-doped CDs/CuBi2O4 photocatalyst was successfully fabricated by loading N-CDs nanoparticles onto the surfaces of CuBi2O4 micro-rods by a facile hydrothermal method, and applied to the photocatalytic water splitting under visible light irradiation. The effect of nitrogen element doped in CDs on the properties of composite was evaluated. The mechanism of photocatalytic hydrogen production of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 was also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and N-CDs powders
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation method of CDs solution is consistent with that of previous report [37]. After filtration, the obtained CDs solution was freeze-dried into solid powder. N-CDs solution was synthesized by ammonia hydrothermal treatment of CDs solution. The specific steps are as follows: the CDs solution and ammonia water are mixed into the reactor at the volume of 1:1. In the oven, the mixed solution was heated to 150 °C for 6 h. Then the reactive solution was filtered for 3 times to obtain the golden N-CDs solution. Finally, the N-CDs solution was freeze-dried into a solid powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuBi2O4, CDs/CuBi2O4 and N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 2.42 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.6 g Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O and 0.87 g NaOH were mixed in 80 mL of distill water and stirred for 3 h. Then, the homogeneous precursor was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected, washed and dried.

For the CDs/CuBi2O4 and N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composites, the 100 mg CDs and N-CDs powders were added into the hydrothermal fabricated process of CuBi2O4. In a typical procedure for synthesizing CDs/CuBi2O4, the 100 mg CDs solid powder was firstly mixed in 80 mL of distill water for 30 min, then 2.42 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.6 g Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O and 0.87 g NaOH were added and stirred for another 2.5 h. Subsequently, the homogeneous precursor was transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling to the room temperature, the product was collected, washed and dried. N-CDs/CuBi2O4 was prepared by the same procedure but changing the CDs solid powder to 100 mg N-CDs. The as-synthesized CDs/CuBi2O4 and N-CDs/CuBi2O4 materials were abbreviated as CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO, respectively. In addition, the pure CuBi2O4 was labeled as CBO.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of materials were recorded using an X‘Pert-ProMPD (Holand) D/max-γA X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). Transmission electron microscope (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) were performed with a FEI-Tecnai F20 microscope operating at 200 kV, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a KRATOS Axis ultra-DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source. UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Lambda 750 (Perking Elmer) spectrophotometer in the tested range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared samples were detected by Fluorolog-TCSPC Luminescence Spectrometer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded by a CHI 920C workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua, Shanghai, China) and using an alternating voltage of 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range of 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz with the open circuit voltage in 0.5 M Na2SO4. For transient photocurrent response, a 300 W Xe lamp (cutting off λ < 420 nm) and Na2SO4 (0.5 M) were employed as the light source and electrolyte, respectively.

The photocatalytic H2 production reactions were carried out in PCX-50C multichannel photochemical reaction device (Beijing Perfect light Co. Ltd., China). The photocatalytic reaction was performed in 18 mL water suspension of photocatalyst (10 mg) with the addition of 2 mL methanol. Then the system was degassed to completely remove air, and irradiated with a white light-emitting diode (LED) as the visible light source (λ > 400 nm) from the bottom of the reactor system. An online GC-7900 gas chromatograph (GC) setup with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and 5 Å molecular sieve columns was employed for evolved gas detection.

Fig. 1 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and CDs in an aqueous solution. Compared with CDs, N-CDs exhibit two characteristic peaks, located at 230 nm and 290 nm, which are attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC bonds and n-π* transition of carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface, respectively [28]. The synthetic N-CDs and CDs were further analyzed and compared by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and CDs display an absorption peak related to the OH group (OH bonds) at 3445 cm−1 and CO/CC bonds at 1638 cm−1. Obviously, in N-CDs spectrum, there are extra vibrational peaks at 1409 cm−1 and 3238 cm−1, which can be attributed to CN and NH [38], respectively. The representative TEM image of N-CDs is presented in Fig. 1c, which disclose near-spherical and well-distributed carbon particles (∼5 nm) with a lattice distance of 0.21 nm, ascribing to the (100) spacing of graphitic carbon [39]. In addition, the TEM image of CDs was also taken and shown in Fig. S1. It is found that CDs exhibit relatively larger sizes (∼8 nm) in comparison with N-CDs (∼5 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to analyze the composition of N-CDs. Fig. 1d shows the full-scan XPS spectrum of N-CDs is main composed of C, N and O elements. The C 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 1e) shows four peaks at 284.4 eV, 284.9 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.2 eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively [40]. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 1f) can be divided into two peaks of 400.4 eV and 401.3 eV, corresponding to CNC and N-(C)3 respectively [41]. In contrast to N-CDs, it is clear that the XPS spectra of CDs contain no corresponding N element in the survey (Fig. S2a) and the functional group of the CN bond in C 1s spectrum (Fig. S2b), respectively. From results mentioned above, we can conclude that the CDs and N-CDs were successfully synthetized, which provide modifiers to further combine the CBO photocatalyst.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO are shown in Fig. 2 . For CBO, all the diffraction peaks can match the standard card of monoclinic CuBi2O4 (JCPDS 72-493) [42]. While for CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO, they exhibit all the featured peaks of monoclinic CuBi2O4 but a weak broad peak of ∼26°, which is mainly attributed to the characteristic peak of carbon [43].

The morphology of the synthesized samples was observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM). As shown in Fig. 3 a, it is observed that the synthesized CBO is rod-like, with a length of about 4–5 μm and an average diameter of 130 nm. Fig. 3b shows the SEM image of N-CDs/CBO. It can be seen from the picture that the introduction of N-CDs does not change the morphology of CBO. However, it is difficult to see N-CDs on the surface of CBO micro-rods. In order to further prove the existence of N-CDs, the N-CDs/CBO samples were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Fig. 3c). It was found that N-CDs loaded on the surface of CBO micro-rods. And the lattice fringe in the HRTEM image can be readily indexed to the interplanar spacing of the (100) crystallographic plane of N-CDs (inset of Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d shows the N-CDs/CBO scanning transmission-high-angle annular dark field (HAADF-SEM) picture and the corresponding element distribution. It demonstrates that the elements of C, N, Cu, Bi and O are distributed in N-CDs/CBO composite. Furthermore, the SEM and TEM images of CDs/CBO with the corresponding elemental mapping are also given in Fig. S3, which demonstrates the successful fabrication of the CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO composite, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was in-depth characterized to determine the composition and chemical states of elements in N-CDs/CBO composites. The full survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a indicates the N-CDs/CBO is composed of C, N, Cu, Bi and O elements. As shown in Fig. 4b, the high-resolution spectra of Cu reveals two binding energies located at 934 eV and 954 eV, which can be ascribed to the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively. And the difference between Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 can be calculated to be 20 eV, which is consistent with the standard spectrum of Cu2+ in Cu-based oxides. Moreover, two satellite peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 located at about 943 eV and 963 eV, attributed to the 3d9 shell of Cu2+ [44]. In the Bi 4f spectrum (Fig. 4c), two fine peaks observed at 158.5 eV and 164 eV are in good accordance with Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively. Meanwhile, the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) can be subdivided into two peaks at 529.7 eV and 531.6 eV, respectively, which are attributed to the lattice oxygen and adsorption oxygen [45]. Fig. 4e and f shows high resolution spectra of C 1s and N 1s, respectively. The corresponding peak positions are consistent with those of C 1s and N 1s in N-CDs (Fig. 1e and f).

The optical properties of the as-synthesized samples were investigated through UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and given in Fig. 5 . It can be seen clearly that pure CBO exhibits outstanding light absorption in both UV and visible regions of solar spectrum. When CBO was combined with CDs and N-CDs, the optical absorption of CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO samples was further enhanced, especially in N-CDs/CBO composites, which was mainly due to the fact that the introduction of N-doped CDs would result in a sub-band gap absorption in the composites [46]. This result shows that N-doped CDs exhibits better light absorption in composite materials than un-doped CDs. Therefore, N-CDs/CBO can produce more photo-induced carriers under visible light irradiation, which can effectively further improve the photocatalytic performance of CBO.

Fig. 6 a shows the time courses of H2 evolution from CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO under visible light irradiation (λ > 400 nm) with or without addition of methanol as a sacrificial electron donor. As can be seen, the CBO micro-rods hardly exhibit photoactivity for H2 evolution during the 24 h produce hydrogen. This result also demonstrates that the photo-generated carriers are easy to recombine when CBO micro-rods is irradiated by visible light. Therefore, pure CBO cannot effectively produce hydrogen in the present photoreaction system. It is worth noting that photocatalytic hydrogen production begins to occur after CDs and N-CDs are modified on the surface of CBO micro-rods. The results showed that the hydrogen production of CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO reached 3.26 μmol and 12.2 μmol during the tested periods, respectively, indicating that the introduction of CDs and N-CDs could promote the positive reaction of hydrogen production. It also shows that N-doped CDs can further enhance the photocatalytic activity of the composites. In order to explore the relationship between N-CDs and CBO, the typical time courses of H2 evolutions over pure N-CDs was also further evaluated and shown in Fig. S4. It can be seen that no gas was produced during the 24 h irradiation, which indicates that the hydrogen production of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite comes from a strong coupling effect between N-CDs and CBO. In addition, the hydrogen cycle stability of N-CDs/CBO samples was tested. The hydrogen production of N-CDS/CBO decreases gradually after four cycles of experiments, which may be due to the oxidative decomposition of methanol (Fig. 6b). According to previous reports, the acidity of the solution increases when methanol (CH3OH) is used as a sacrificial agent, producing a small amount of formic acid (HCOOH) and CO2 [5]. A small amount of formic acid may react with CBO, resulting in partial dissolution of CBO, resulting in a decrease in the activity of the catalyst. To verify this, the pH of the solution before and after the reaction was compared. The results showed that the pH of the solution decreased from 7.1 to 6.9, which indicated that a small amount of HCOOH and CO2 were produced in the photocatalytic system of N-CDs/CBO.

Generally, photoluminescence (PL) analysis is closely related to the migration, transfer and separation efficiency of the photoexcited electrons and holes in semiconductors [47,48]. The more the recombination of electron hole is, the stronger the PL intensity is [49,50]. Fig. 7 a displays the PL spectra of CBO, CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO with the excitation at 325 nm. As can be seen that the three samples exhibit two main characteristic peaks at 380 nm and 410 nm. In addition, we can also observe that although the fluorescence emission peaks of the three samples are similar, the fluorescence intensity is slightly different. Compared with pure CBO, the peak intensity of CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO are slightly weaker, which indicates that the introduction of CDs and N-CDs can effectively hinder the recombination of photo-generated carriers. In order to verify the electron transfer efficiency of the samples, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of CBO CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO were measured. Theoretically, the radius of the impedance spectrum arc can be used to measure the charge transfer impedance of the electrode surface. In general, a smaller semi-circular arc means faster charge transfer efficiency at the interface of photocatalyst [51,52]. In Fig. 7b, it can be seen from that the arc radius of CDs/CBO and N-CDs/CBO samples is smaller than that of CBO, among which the radius of N-CDs/CBO is the smallest, which indicates that the photocatalytic activity of this material is optimum because of the fast separation and transfer rate of photo-generated carriers. Additionally, the photocurrent responses of as-prepared samples (Fig. S5) were also tested to study the charge carrier separation capacities of photocatalysts and a larger photocurrent expresses the higher efficiency of the highly separated electron-hole pairs [53]. It can be also inferred that N-doped CDs has better electron transport efficiency than un-doped CDs in the composite photocatalytic materials.

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the possible mechanism of photodissociation of N-CDs/CBO for hydrogen production under visible light was discussed in detail (Fig. 8 ). Firstly, when N-CDs/CBO composite is irradiated by visible light, it can absorb photons and produce photo-induced electrons and holes, which are located on CB and VB of CBO, respectively. Secondly, because N-CDs exhibits better electron transport efficiency in composite photocatalytic materials, electrons located on CB can be effectively transferred to N-CDs, thus H2O can be reduced to H2, and at the same time, the hole in VB is oxidized with CH3OH to form HCOOH, which can be further oxidized into CO2 and H2O by hole oxidation.

A novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) modified CuBi2O4 photocatalyst was prepared for photocatalytic water splitting. The N-CDs nanoparticles were anchored onto the surfaces of CuBi2O4 micro-rods. The hydrogen production result reveals that N-CDs/CuBi2O4 shows enhancing photocatalytic activity compared to the pristine CuBi2O4 and CDs/CuBi2O4. The excellent photocatalytic performance of N-CDs/CuBi2O4 composite was attributed to the introduction of N-CDs in composite increased light harvesting and electron transfer capacity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Zhangjiagang Science and Technology Support Program (Social Development) of Jiangsu Province, China (grant numbers: ZKS1510).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2018.10.095.
